note_data.title: Group 2
keyPoints: Group 2 elements are **alkaline earth metals** with electronic configuration ns² where n is the outer shell
Group 2 elements show **metallic bonding** and form **ionic compounds** with oxidation state +2
**Atomic radius increases** down Group 2 due to additional electron shells
**First ionisation energy decreases** down Group 2 as outer electrons are further from nucleus and more shielded
**Reactivity increases** down Group 2 because ionisation energies decrease making electron loss easier
**Thermal stability of carbonates increases** down Group 2: MgCO₃ < CaCO₃ < SrCO₃ < BaCO₃
**Thermal stability of nitrates increases** down Group 2 following the same pattern as carbonates
Group 2 hydroxides become **more soluble** down the group: Mg(OH)₂ < Ca(OH)₂ < Sr(OH)₂ < Ba(OH)₂
Group 2 sulfates become **less soluble** down the group: MgSO₄ > CaSO₄ > SrSO₄ > BaSO₄
**Flame test colours**: Mg - no colour, Ca - brick red, Sr - crimson red, Ba - green

definitions.definition: Group 2 elements that form alkaline solutions when their oxides react with water
Breaking down of a compound by heat into two or more simpler substances
The ability of a cation to distort the electron cloud of an anion
The enthalpy change when one mole of an ionic solid is formed from gaseous ions under standard conditions

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Use trends to suggest properties of unknown Group 2 compounds
State similarities and differences with specific examples

modelAnswer.question: Explain why the thermal stability of Group 2 carbonates increases down the group. [3]
modelAnswer.answer: Cation charge density decreases down Group 2. This is because ionic radius increases while charge remains +2. Smaller, more highly charged cations have greater polarising power and distort the carbonate ion more, making decomposition easier.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Cation charge density decreases down the group
Mark 2: Ionic radius increases while charge stays the same
Mark 3: Higher charge density causes greater polarisation of carbonate ion

commonMistakes: Never say 'atomic size' — always say 'atomic radius' or 'ionic radius'
Students often confuse solubility trends: hydroxides increase, sulfates decrease down Group 2
Don't say 'stability increases because atoms get bigger' — explain using charge density and polarising power
Never write M²⁺ in equations — always specify the actual metal (Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺, etc.)
Students forget that MgCO₃ and Mg(NO₃)₂ decompose at relatively low temperatures compared to other Group 2 compounds
examinerTips: Thermal stability questions appear frequently - learn the charge density explanation
Always give specific examples when discussing trends (e.g. Mg vs Ba)
Solubility questions often test both hydroxides and sulfates - learn both trends
Flame tests are commonly tested - memorise colours exactly as given in syllabus
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Born-Haber cycle showing lattice enthalpy trends, or apparatus for thermal decomposition of carbonates with gas syringe to collect CO₂
pastPaperRefs: M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain the trend in reactivity of Group 2 elements with water and write balanced equations for magnesium and calcium reactions
O/N 2022 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — multiple choice question testing knowledge of Group 2 hydroxide solubility trend from Mg(OH)₂ to Ba(OH)₂
M/J 2022 P2 Q3(c) (4 marks) — required explanation of why barium sulfate is used as a contrast agent in X-rays, linking to its insolubility and non-toxicity
O/N 2023 P2 Q5 (5 marks) — asked to compare the thermal decomposition of Group 2 carbonates, explaining why BaCO₃ requires higher temperature than MgCO₃

workedExample.question: Explain the trend in solubility of Group 2 sulfates down the group. Write the ionic equation for the reaction between barium chloride and sodium sulfate. (5 marks)
steps: Step 1: State the trend - Group 2 sulfate solubility decreases down the group
Step 2: Give examples - MgSO₄ is soluble, CaSO₄ is slightly soluble, SrSO₄ and BaSO₄ are insoluble
Step 3: Explain the trend - as cation size increases down the group, lattice energy decreases less than hydration energy, making sulfates less soluble
Step 4: Write the ionic equation - Ba²⁺(aq) + SO₄²⁻(aq) → BaSO₄(s)
Step 5: State observation - white precipitate forms
workedExample.answer: Group 2 sulfate solubility decreases down the group. MgSO₄ is soluble, CaSO₄ is slightly soluble, while SrSO₄ and BaSO₄ are insoluble. This occurs because as cation size increases, the decrease in lattice energy is less significant than the decrease in hydration energy. Ba²⁺(aq) + SO₄²⁻(aq) → BaSO₄(s). A white precipitate of barium sulfate forms.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for trend direction, 1 mark for examples, 2 marks for explanation involving lattice/hydration energy, 1 mark for correct ionic equation

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the solubility trends of Group 2 hydroxides and sulfates' — always remember hydroxides become MORE soluble down the group while sulfates become LESS soluble

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently wrote molecular equations instead of ionic equations for precipitation reactions' — always write ionic equations for sulfate tests showing only the reacting ions and precipitate formed

Examiner Report 2023: 'Weak explanations of reactivity trends often missed the key concept of ionization energy' — always mention that reactivity increases down Group 2 because first and second ionization energies decrease, making electron loss easier
note_data.keyDiagram: Reactivity trend diagram showing Group 2 elements (Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba) with vertical arrows indicating: atomic radius increases downward, ionization energy decreases downward, reactivity with water increases downward. Include observations: Mg - very slow reaction with steam, Ca - steady bubbling with cold water, Sr - vigorous bubbling, Ba - very vigorous reaction. Show relative positions and trends clearly with labeled arrows.
note_data.quickRecap: Group 2 elements become more reactive down the group as ionization energies decrease. Hydroxides become more soluble (Mg(OH)₂ insoluble → Ba(OH)₂ soluble) while sulfates become less soluble (MgSO₄ soluble → BaSO₄ insoluble). All form 2+ ions and react with water to produce hydrogen gas. Thermal stability of carbonates and nitrates increases down the group due to decreasing charge density of cations.
